title: Thresholds Versus Fractional Expectation-Thresholds
speaker: Keith Frankston
abstract: Given an increasing family F in {0,1}^n, its measure according to mu_p increases and often exhibits a threshold behavior, growing quickly as p increases from near 0 to near 1 around a specific value p_c. Thresholds of families have been of great historical interest and a central focus of the study of random discrete structures (e.g. random graphs and hypergraphs), with estimation of thresholds for specific properties the subject of some of the most challenging work in the area.

proof_stages.label: Threshold framework for increasing families
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up increasing families of subsets of a finite ground set, the product random subset X_p with law μ_p, and the threshold p_c defined by μ_p(F)=1/2.

proof_stages.label: Expectation thresholds from first-moment certificates
proof_stages.summary: Using first-moment reasoning, the speaker defines p-smallness and q(F) as the best lower bound on p_c obtainable from weighted collections of witnesses, leading to the Kahn–Kalai logarithmic-threshold conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Fractional expectation threshold and proved conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The witness definition is relaxed to nonnegative fractional weights, defining weak p-smallness and q_f(F), and the main announced result is that the Kahn–Kalai type upper bound holds for this fractional threshold.

proof_stages.label: Application targets motivating the theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states two motivating consequences—Shamir's one-factor problem in random uniform hypergraphs and thresholds for fixed bounded-degree spanning trees in G(n,p)—as applications where the fractional version is strong enough.

proof_stages.label: Spread formulation and duality connection
proof_stages.summary: A multi-hypergraph H of minimal witnesses is called ρ-spread, the main theorem says a random set of size about ρ|X|log|X| contains a member of H with high probability, and LP duality identifies this spread condition with the fractional expectation threshold.

proof_stages.label: Spread computations for applications
proof_stages.summary: For perfect matchings, r-uniform one-factors, and fixed bounded-degree spanning trees, choosing H as the family of minimal copies and estimating its spread immediately gives the desired threshold upper bounds.

proof_stages.label: Incremental reduction lemma strategy
proof_stages.summary: The proof of the main spread theorem exposes the random set in increments and repeatedly replaces H by smaller residual witnesses while preserving spread except for a small discarded bad part.

proof_stages.label: Counting bad pairs to preserve spread
proof_stages.summary: A counting argument bounds the number of bad pairs (S,W) by specifying Z=S∪W, the image S′, a large intersection T, then using spread to show bad pairs are overwhelmingly rarer than all pairs, enabling logarithmically many reductions.

examples.name: Erdős–Rényi graph as a random subset
examples.note: Taking X to be the edge set of the complete graph makes X_p equal to G(n,p), concretizing the abstract threshold notation for increasing families.
examples.engagement_reason: Identifies G(n,p) as the special case where the ground set is the pairs and X_p is a random graph, but does no computation on this example itself.

examples.name: Perfect matching threshold in G(n,p)
examples.note: The contrast between the first-moment lower bound p≈1/n and the isolated-vertex barrier p≈(log n)/n motivates the logarithmic gap behind expectation-threshold conjectures, and later the same object illustrates the spread calculation.
examples.engagement_reason: Computes the expected number of perfect matchings, discusses the isolated-vertex obstruction, formulates the family of graphs containing a perfect matching, and performs the spread calculation to get rho about e/n and the threshold order.

passes.reason: a perfect matching is a spanning maximum matching with regular one-factor structure, and it is used to show the naive first-moment threshold misses the true log-factor threshold
A perfect matching is a maximum spanning matching, and the example is used to show the naive first-moment threshold can be too low by a logarithmic factor.
A perfect matching is a maximally sized 1-factor covering every vertex, used as the orienting threshold example rather than to refute a claim.
A perfect matching is a spanning maximum 1-regular matching, and the example shows the naive first-moment threshold misses the isolate barrier by a logarithm.
A perfect matching is a maximally large matching covering all vertices, and here it illustrates the failure of the naive first-moment scale due to isolated vertices.
a perfect matching is a maximum 1-factor and the example is used to show the naive first-moment threshold can be logarithmically too small
The perfect-matching threshold is an orienting application, and a perfect matching/threshold is extremal as a spanning maximum matching and critical probability.
Used to show the naive first-moment threshold is off by a log factor, with perfect matchings being spanning maximum matchings.
A perfect matching is a spanning maximal matching, and its threshold is used to show the naive first-moment expectation scale misses a logarithmic isolate barrier.
It shows the naive first-moment threshold prediction fails because isolated vertices force the critical perfect-matching threshold to Θ(log n/n).

the perfect-matching threshold is used to show the naive first-moment heuristic fails since isolated vertices force the larger log n/n scale

examples.name: Shamir's problem for one-factors in random r-uniform hypergraphs
examples.note: The one-factor problem is presented as a major application where the general theorem recovers the n^{1-r} log n threshold by the same spread computation as for perfect matchings, with 2 replaced by r.
examples.engagement_reason: Defines the r-uniform one-factor problem, derives the isolated-vertex lower-bound order, and applies the same spread calculation with 2 changed to r to obtain the threshold order.

examples.name: Fixed bounded-degree spanning tree in G(n,p)
examples.note: A spanning tree of maximum degree at most a fixed d is used as an application showing that the theorem gives the log n/n threshold with explicit linear dependence on d.
examples.engagement_reason: For a fixed bounded-degree spanning tree, chooses h to be the copies of the tree and states the resulting spread calculation rho at most de/n, yielding the threshold bound.

examples.name: Universal containment of all bounded-degree spanning trees
examples.note: The universal version of Montgomery's theorem is mentioned to clarify both the strength of known results and the limitation that the spread method would require understanding minimal universal graphs.
examples.engagement_reason: Describes universality as the stronger property and notes that the minimal universal graphs are not understood, but does not carry out a spread calculation or prove a threshold for it.

passes.reason: universal containment demands covering the entire family of bounded-degree spanning trees, but it is mentioned as a stronger target/limitation rather than as a counterexample
